Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Only right-side bars were removed (24 bars). Left-side bars (20 bars) remain. The clicks on left-side bars apparently didn't work. Need to investigate why and try again.
- Plan: Try clicking a left-side bar directly to see if it can be removed. If not, the puzzle might require a different approach.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed
before_seg = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
after_seg = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation

# Count gray nodes
before_w = [n for n in before_seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'w']
after_w = [n for n in after_seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'w']
print(f"Gray nodes before: {len(before_w)}, after: {len(after_w)}")

# Check all nodes for changes
before_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in before_seg['nodes']}
after_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in after_seg['nodes']}

# Find changed nodes
for nid in after_nodes:
    if nid in before_nodes:
        bn = before_nodes[nid]
        an = after_nodes[nid]
        if bn['color'] != an['color'] or bn['pixels'] != an['pixels']:
            print(f"Node {nid} changed: color {bn['color']}->{an['color']}, pixels {bn['pixels']}->{an['pixels']}")
    else:
        print(f"Node {nid} appeared: color={after_nodes[nid]['color']}")

for nid in before_nodes:
    if nid not in after_nodes:
        print(f"Node {nid} disappeared: color={before_nodes[nid]['color']}")

# Check the ascii around row 36, col 8
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"\nRow 36 around col 8: '{ascii_lines[36][6:12]}'")
print(f"Row 36 full: '{ascii_lines[36]}'")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Gray nodes before: 20, after: 20
Node 1 changed: color b->b, pixels 15->14
Node 2 changed: color G->G, pixels 46->47

Row 36 around col 8: 'ggwggg'
Row 36 full: 'BgggggggwggggwggggwggggwgggggggBWWWWWWWBWWWWBWWWWBWWWWBWWWWWWWBB'

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The bar is still there (20 gray nodes before and after). But node 1 changed from 15 to 14 pixels, and node 2 changed from 46 to 47 pixels. Node 1 is the blue bar at the top (the timer/progress bar), and node 2 is the dark gray (G) background.
So clicking the left-side bar didn't remove it - it just changed the timer bar. This means the left-side bars are NOT removable. They're probably just decorative or represent something else.
Wait, but the right-side bars were removed. Let me think about why the left-side bars can't be removed but the right-side bars can.
Looking at the image:
- Left panel: gray background with bars on it
- Right panel: white background with bars on it
Maybe the bars on the left side are part of the gray background and can't be removed, while the bars on the right side are on a white background and can be removed.
But wait, the bars are all the same color (w = light gray). The difference is the background: left side has gray (g) background, right side has white (W) background.
Actually, looking at the ASCII output for row 36:
'ggggggggwggggwggggwggggwgggggggBWWWWWWWBWWWWBWWWWBWWWWBWWWWWWWBB'
The left side has 'g' (gray) background with 'w' (light gray) bars, and the right side has 'W' (white) background with 'B' (black) gaps where bars were removed.
So the right-side bars were successfully removed, but the left-side bars can't be removed (they're on a different background).
But the level isn't complete yet. What else do I need to do?
Let me think about the bottom boxes again. Maybe I need to click on them or interact with them in some way.
Looking at the bottom boxes:
- Left blue box: has yellow dots and white dots
- Right gray box: has a yellow square and white dots
Maybe I need to click on the yellow elements in the boxes to "activate" them, which then allows me to remove the left-side bars.
Or maybe the level is complete once all removable bars are removed, and I just need to click something else to advance.
Let me try clicking on the yellow elements in the bottom boxes.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Left-side bars can't be removed (clicking them only changes the timer). Right-side bars were successfully removed. Level still not complete. Need to interact with bottom boxes or other elements.
Plan: Try clicking the yellow elements in the bottom boxes to see if they trigger something.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Find yellow nodes in bottom boxes
seg = current_frame.segmentation
yellow_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'Y']
print(f"Yellow nodes: {len(yellow_nodes)}")
for n in yellow_nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    center_row = sum(rows) // len(rows)
    center_col = sum(cols) // len(cols)
    print(f"  Node {n['id']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, center=({center_row}, {center_col}), boundary={b}")

# The yellow nodes in bottom boxes are:
# Node 110: yellow square in right gray box (rows 55-56, cols 20-22)
# Node 111: yellow dots in left blue box (rows 57-59, cols 8-9)
# Let me try clicking on node 110 first (yellow square)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 55, 'col': 21}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
